main_sections.title: Try Again
main_sections.poem_text:
King Bruce of Scotland flung himself down
In a lonely mood to think;
'Tis true he was monarch, and wore a crown,
But his heart was beginning to sink.

For he had been trying to do a great deed,
To make his people glad;
He had tried and tried, but couldn't succeed
And so he became quite sad.

He flung himself down in low despair,
As grieved as man could be;
And after a while he pondered there,
"I'll give it all up," said he.

Now just at that moment a spider dropped,
With its silken, filmy clue;
And the King, in the midst of his thinking, stopped
To see what the spider would do.

'Twas a long way up to the ceiling dome,
And it hung by a rope so fine;
That how it would get to its cobweb home,
King Bruce could not divine.

It soon began to cling and crawl
Straight up with strong endeavour;
But down it came with a slippery sprawl,
As near to the ground as ever.

Up, up it ran, not a second to stay,
To utter the least complaint;
Till it fell still lower, and there it lay,
A little dizzy and faint.

Its head grew steady — again it went,
And travelled a half-yard higher;
'Twas a delicate thread it had to tread,
And a road where its feet would tire.

Again it fell and swung below,
But again it quickly mounted;
Till up and down, now fast, now slow,
Nine brave attempts were counted.

"Sure," cried the King, "that foolish thing
Will strive no more to climb;
When it toils so hard to reach and cling,
And tumbles every time."

But up the insect went once more,
Ah me! 'tis an anxious minute;
He's only a foot from his cobweb door,
Oh say, will he lose or win it?

Steadily, steadily, inch by inch,
Higher and higher he got;
And a bold little run at the very last pinch
Put him into his native cot.

"Bravo, bravo!" the King cried out,
"All honour to those who try;
The spider up there, defied despair;
He conquered, and why shouldn't I?"

And Bruce of Scotland braced his mind,
And gossips tell the tale,
That he tried once more as he tried before,
And that time did not fail.

Eliza Cook
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Written by Eliza Cook in a narrative ballad style with an ABAB rhyme scheme, 'Try Again' recounts the legend of King Bruce of Scotland who, despairing after repeated failures to accomplish a great deed for his people, watches a spider attempt nine times to climb to its cobweb home before finally succeeding. The spider serves as an extended metaphor for perseverance — its every stumble and recovery mirroring Bruce's own repeated defeats. The dominant tone is one of quiet, mounting suspense that breaks into celebration and renewed resolve. Central imagery includes the 'silken, filmy clue' of the spider's thread, the 'ceiling dome' as an aspirational height, and 'steadily, steadily, inch by inch' as the rhythm of determined effort. The poem's chief device is repetition — 'tried and tried', 'up, up', 'steadily, steadily' — which enacts the very persistence it celebrates. The closing stanza resolves both story threads: Bruce, inspired by the creature, tries once more and succeeds. The universal message is that failure is not final; perseverance converts despair into triumph.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. I Who inspires you to do your best?

question_bank.stem: Who inspires you to do your best?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. II Work in pairs, identify what you see in the pictures given below (honeybee hive, spider web, geese flying, weaver bird nest, ants working, apple tree). What can we learn from them? Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Identify what you see in the pictures given below. What can we learn from them?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — I. Complete the summary of the poem by filling in the blanks with suitable words from the text given in the box below. Box: tried  mounted  despair  attempts  great  conquered  low  strive

question_bank.stem: Complete the summary of the poem by filling in the blanks with suitable words from the box: The poem, written in a narrative style, tells the story of King Bruce of Scotland, who was feeling 1. _____________ after failing multiple times to achieve something 2. _____________ for his people. In a moment of 3. _____________, he observed a spider trying to reach its web high above. The spider 4. _____________ every time it fell after getting close to its goal. It made nine 5. _____________ and finally succeeded. The king said that the spider had 6. _____________ and so would he. Inspired by the spider's determination, King Bruce decided to 7. _____________. When he 8. _____________ this time, he ultimately succeeded.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — II. Complete the following sentences suitably.

question_bank.stem: We can say that the poet uses the narrative style because ________.
The central idea of the poem is ________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — III. Pick three examples from the poem for each of the following.

question_bank.stem: Pick three examples from the poem for: lines that describe the spider's efforts.
Pick three examples from the poem for: alliteration (the repetition of the same consonant sounds at the beginning of closely connected words).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — IV. Why does the poet repeat the following words or phrases in the poem? tried and tried; steadily, steadily; up, up

question_bank.stem: Why does the poet repeat the following words or phrases in the poem? tried and tried; steadily, steadily; up, up

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — V. Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct answer from within the brackets.

question_bank.stem: The rhyme scheme of the poem is ____________. (AABB/ABBA/ABAB)
'Bravo' is an example of ____________. (conjunction/interjection/adjective)
The poet uses 'twas and 'tis for the sake of ____________. (rhythm/rhyme/contraction)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — VI.1. A metaphor is a figure of speech that involves an implied comparison between two unlike things without using 'like', 'as', or 'as...as'. The spider's climbing is compared to King Bruce's own struggles — this poetic device is called metaphor. Read the following sentences and identify metaphors and similes. Write M for metaphor and S for simile.

question_bank.stem: Read the following sentences and identify metaphors and similes. Write M for metaphor and S for simile: (i) He ran as fast as a cheetah. (ii) The world is a stage. (iii) The night was as dark as coal. (iv) He sang like an angel. (v) Her voice was music to his ears. (vi) The classroom was a zoo. (vii) The baby slept like a log. (viii) Her smile was as bright as the sun. (ix) The lake was a mirror, reflecting the sky.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — VI.2. Read the following sentences and transform the similes to metaphors and metaphors to similes. Two examples have been given.

question_bank.stem: Transform the similes to metaphors and metaphors to similes: (iii) The book was a treasure chest, filled with endless adventures. (iv) Her laughter was a melody that brightened the darkest days. (v) The mountain stood as a guard, watching over the valley below. (vi) The night was like a velvet cloak, wrapping the world in mystery. (vii) Her thoughts were like butterflies in a summer meadow.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 1: 'He flung himself down in low despair...With its silken, filmy clue;' Answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: Select the phrase which shows the physical expression of the King's emotional state.
Why does the poet use the word 'low' before despair?
Choose the correct option to complete the sentence. In the line, 'And after a while he pondered there', the word 'pondered' means _____________________. A. paused  B. thought  C. noticed  D. rested
Choose the correct option from the words given in the brackets. The phrase 'silken filmy clue' creates an image of something that is _____________________ (long and light/very delicate and soft).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 2: '...when it toils so hard to reach and cling, And tumbles every time.' / 'But up the insect went once more...' Answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: Choose the correct option to complete the analogy. toil: hard::_______________: _______________ A. roll: tumble  B. tumble: circle  C. compress: roll  D. jump: tumble
List any two characteristics of the spider highlighted in the line, 'But up the insect went once more'.
Choose the correct option to complete the sentence. The expression 'Ah me!' in the extract indicates a sense of _______________. A. happiness  B. stress  C. relief  D. loneliness
Complete the sentence with an appropriate reason. Readers are able to relate to this extract because ____________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: How does the first stanza help in setting the mood of the poem?
Describe how King Bruce's attitude changes from the beginning towards the end of the poem.
The poet describes every action of the spider in great detail. What does this tell us about his attitude towards the spider?
How does the spider inspire us to overcome despair and not give up?
The poem teaches us that failures are stepping stones to success. Explain.
The spider's journey tells us that anyone can be a source of inspiration in our lives. Elaborate.

listening.transcript_text: In a thick green forest lived a little ant, known for her determination and never-give-up attitude. One sunny day she found a piece of roti too big to carry alone. 'This crumb is huge, but I must get it back to the anthill. My family will be so happy!' She tried to lift it with all her might, but it was too heavy. She pushed, pulled and rolled it, but nothing worked. Exhausted and saddened, she sat down and cried. Then she noticed a small bird trying to build a nest. It picked up twigs and leaves, but the wind kept blowing them away. The bird did not give up; it kept trying until it built a strong nest. 'If that bird can keep trying and succeed, then I shouldn't give up either,' the ant thought, feeling inspired. Determined once more, she called her fellow ants for help. Together they formed a chain and lifted the crumb with their combined strength, carrying it slowly back to the anthill. 'We did it! We showed that with teamwork and never giving up, we can achieve anything!' They celebrated their success. We too should learn never to give up, no matter how difficult the situation may seem.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I You will listen to a story about an ant. Read the questions given below and before you listen, guess the answers to these questions.

question_bank.stem: What was the ant known for? (i) determination  (ii) strength  (iii) wisdom
Why was the ant unable to take the roti home? (i) It was stuck under a rock.  (ii) It was too heavy to carry.  (iii) It was pulled by other ants.
options: It was stuck under a rock.
It was too heavy to carry.
It was pulled by other ants.

question_bank.stem: What was the bird doing? (i) playing with the ant  (ii) searching for food  (iii) picking up leaves
How did the bird make the ant feel? (i) proud  (ii) inspired  (iii) relaxed
How did the fellow ants help? (i) by advising the ant continuously  (ii) by standing still near the ant  (iii) by joining the ant in her efforts
options: by standing still near the ant
by joining the ant in her efforts

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Now, listen to the story once again and as you listen, check whether your answers are correct.

Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Your perseverance helped you. Use the following sentence prompts as a guide for your narration: 1. Introduction  2. Describe the challenge  3. Feeling disheartened  4. The source of motivation  5. Taking action  6. The result  7. Reflection.

question_bank.stem: Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Use the sentence prompts as a guide.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: King Bruce was inspired by the spider that kept on trying till it was successful. Think about the steps you can take to overcome a difficult situation and write them down. Now, write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation. Also, advise not to quit and persevere. Remember to use transition words like 'To begin with…,' 'Next…,' 'After that…,' 'Then...,' 'Finally...,' to share the steps of your plan.

question_bank.stem: Write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation. Advise not to quit and persevere. Use transition words: 'To begin with…,' 'Next…,' 'After that…,' 'Then...,' 'Finally...' Begin: '20, Rajendra Nagar / Jeevanpur / 23 August 20XX / Dear Monika, / Thank you for your letter asking me about…' End: 'Yours affectionately, / Deepa'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Write the opposites of the following words taken from the poem.

question_bank.stem: Write the opposites of the following words taken from the poem: 1. up x _____________  2. fast x _____________  3. glad x _____________  4. win x _____________  5. succeed x _____________

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Choose the correct meaning of the underlined words in the following sentences from the box given below. Frame sentences of your own for these words. Box meanings: 1. work very hard and/or for a long time  2. prepared themselves for something difficult  3. an attempt to do something new or difficult  4. to try very hard to achieve something

question_bank.stem: Choose the correct meaning of the underlined words from the box and frame sentences of your own: (i) Please make every endeavour to arrive on time. (ii) Ants toil tirelessly to save food for rainy season. (iii) We encourage all students to strive to do their best. (iv) The sailors braced themselves against the strong wind.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III The poem uses words that show distance. For example: a long way up, inch by inch, higher and higher, half-yard higher. Classify the words or phrases in the box given below based on the distance (far or near). Box: proximity  middle of nowhere  vicinity  adjacent  remoteness  yonder  afar  a stone's throw  light year  hairline  farther

question_bank.stem: Classify the words or phrases based on the distance (far or near): proximity / middle of nowhere / vicinity / adjacent / remoteness / yonder / afar / a stone's throw / light year / hairline / farther

tasks_verbatim.task_text: IV Read the following line from the poem: ''Twas a delicate thread it had to tread,' Now repeat the lines five to six times. Are you able to say it clearly? Create more tongue twisters of your own.

question_bank.stem: Read and repeat the tongue twister: ''Twas a delicate thread it had to tread.' Create more tongue twisters of your own.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Collect folk songs in your own language that connect us with nature and compile them as a class project.

question_bank.stem: Collect folk songs in your own language that connect us with nature and compile them as a class project.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Did you know that spiders are not insects? They belong to a group called the arachnids. Observe a spider and an ant from a distance and note down the differences between them. Share your observations with your classmates. Ask your Science teacher for more information to know about spiders better.

question_bank.stem: Observe a spider and an ant from a distance and note down the differences between them. Share your observations with your classmates.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III Let us learn how to weave a spider web. 1. Take three sticks (each about eight inches long) and a roll of old wool. 2. Tie the sticks together in the centre with the wool (Fig. 1). 3. Start weaving the web by putting a loop around every stick (Figs. 2 and 3). 4. Complete the web and tie the thread in a knot on the stick where the web is complete (Fig. 4). Now, decorate it with words and phrases you learnt about the spider in the poem or anything else that you like.

question_bank.stem: Weave a spider web using sticks and wool following the four steps given. Decorate it with words and phrases from the poem.
